data.title: Sam Altman’s rival to Neuralink reportedly eying gene therapy technology
data.description:
A novel gene therapy idea is reportedly among the focus areas considered by Merge Labs, a new brain-computer interface startup positioned by its backers, including Sam Altman and his AI firm OpenAI, as a potential rival to Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk’s neurotechnology company Neuralink.

Merge Labs is evaluating an experimental gene therapy approach designed to alter brain cells, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the plans.

The people, who were not authorized to speak publicly about the plans, added that the company is also looking at the idea of developing an ultrasound device that can be implanted on the head and then used to sense and control the activity of modified brain cells.

On Wednesday, the Financial Times reported that Merge Labs was planning to raise roughly $250M at a valuation of about $850M, with a substantial portion of that funding expected to come from Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed OpenAI.

Altman will co-found the venture but was not expected to commit his personal funds to the business and is unlikely to be involved in its daily operations, according to the FT report.

Having co-founded OpenAI in 2015, Musk and Altman had a public falling out, leading to the former’s departure from the company’s board three years later. With Merge Labs, their fierce rivalry in the AI space is expected to extend to the field of brain-computer interface.

data.title: Wall Street is mixed as investors await Trump-Putin summit in Alaska
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages are mixed on Friday, as market participants are keeping an eye on the highly anticipated meeting between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) trades higher by 0.3%, while the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is lower by 0.1%. At the same time, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite is down by 0.4%.

From a sector vantage point, six of the S&P 500’s 11 segments are currently trading in positive territory, with Health Care and Real Estate at the top of the leaderboard. In reverse, Financials are the weakest performer in the session so far. 

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields are up as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) pushed up 4 basis point to 4.33%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) is higher by 2 basis points to 3.75%. 

Later today, attention will turn to Alaska, where Trump and Putin are slated to meet for talks expected to focus on Ukraine. En route to the summit, Trump stated on the possibility of territorial swaps: 

"They'll be discussed, but I've got to let Ukraine make that decision... I'm not here to negotiate for Ukraine. I'm here to get them at a table." 

On the economic calendar, July retail sales rose slightly less than expected and core sales came in line with forecasts. Import prices rose more than expected in July, and the export prices inched up. 

Industrial production edged down in July while manufacturing output remained unchanged.

Additionally, the U.S. consumer sentiment index slid to 58.6 in August from 61.7 in July and business inventories rose, as expected, in the month of June.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of UnitedHealth (UNH) popped more than 12% after Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway disclosed in a regulatory filing that it purchased more than 5M shares of the insurer last quarter. 

Applied Materials (AMAT), meanwhile, tumbled over 12% after delivering fourth-quarter guidance that came in well below Wall Street’s expectations.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: COHR, AMGN, KKR, SOFI
data.description:
Coherent Corp’s (COHR) outlook has led to a re-evaluation of its investment potential following its Q4 report, with analyst Chris Lau seeing opportunity despite the market’s negative reaction. Meanwhile, SoFi Technologies (SOFI) and KKR & Co. (KKR) face rating downgrades as analysts cite valuation concerns despite strong operational performance.

Upgrades

  • Coherent Corp. (COHR): Upgrade Rating by Chris Lau. Despite a 20% stock drop after Q4 results, the analyst sees the reaction as overblown, highlighting strong growth potential in AI datacenters and new product segments.

    “Impatient investors are punishing Coherent for its conservative guidance. The underlying business momentum did not change. Management set realistic goals for the timing of the sales acceleration this year through 2026.”

  • Amgen Inc. (AMGN): Upgrade from Hold to Buy by Envision Research. Analysis reveals Amgen is better positioned than Johnson & Johnson to navigate drug pricing pressures with stronger dividend growth and valuation metrics.

    “After a holistic comparison, I consider AMGN to be the better-positioned one. Amgen’s forward dividend yield of 3.29% surpasses JNJ’s 2.98% by a good margin, providing better downside protection. Furthermore, Amgen has also demonstrated more robust dividend growth rates in recent years, with a three-year compounded annual growth rate of 7.76% compared to JNJ’s 5.21%.”

Downgrades

  • SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI): Downgrade from Strong Buy to Hold by Dair Sansyzbayev. Despite being a significant winner for the analyst with a 265% gain over 12 months, current valuation metrics and competitive pressures from companies like Upstart suggest caution.

    “Valuation looks too aggressive at the moment, even despite SOFI’s stellar fundamental potential. Seasonality for August-September does not look good either. Competition from Upstart might be a problem if they continue delivering such aggressive revenue growth.”

  • KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR): Downgrade from Buy to Hold by Seeking Profits. While recent regulatory changes could benefit KKR by allowing it to sell private products into 401(k) plans, the stock’s 30% gain over the past year has pushed it toward fair value.

    “With modest upside, I do not see KKR as a sell, but it is no longer a buy. With shares around fair value, I am moving shares down to a ‘hold.’ I would emphasize that while there is now a regulatory greenlight, firms like KKR will need approval from plan sponsors, which can take time.”

data.title: Williams upgraded at CIBC as best positioned for positive trends in natural gas demand
data.description:
Williams (NYSE:WMB) +1.5% in Friday's trading as CIBC upgraded the energy infrastructure company to Outperform from Neutral with a $64 price target, raised from $61, citing increasingly bullish natural gas demand trends and the outlook for infrastructure.

Williams (NYSE:WMB) provides excellent exposure to North American natural gas fundamentals, such as AI, electrification, and LNG demand, and is the best positioned stock in CIBC's coverage to benefit from the positive trends, analyst Robert Catellier said, adding that some modest price weakness provides investors with an opportunity to enter the stock.

While Williams (WMB) itself has experienced steady increases in firm transmission capacity and G&P volumes in various natural gas price environments, Catellier said the company's significant asset incumbency supports it having the fastest medium-term growth rate of 5%-7%.

The analyst is optimistic about the company in its own right, but he also sees potential weakness in oil prices, further supporting his view that investors should own gas-weighted equities such as Williams (WMB).

Catellier also notes key basins where Williams (WMB) has exposure, such as the Haynesville, have been resilient, seeing a 28% Y/Y increase in rigs, and despite some slowdown in drilling activity in oil-directed basins such as the Permian, the gas-to-oil ratios have improved, although Williams has less exposure to the Permian.

data.title: Federal Reserve to end Biden-era oversight program for banks' crypto activities
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is shutting down its Biden-era initiative for monitoring banks' crypto activity, it said on Friday, moving oversight bank into the regular supervisory process.

“Since the Board started its program to supervise certain crypto and fintech activities in banks, the Board has strengthened its understanding of those activities, related risks, and bank risk management practices,” the central bank said in a statement.

“As a result, the Board is integrating that knowledge and the supervision of those activities back into the standard supervisory process and is rescinding its 2023 supervisory letter creating the program,” it added.

Under the Biden administration, the program was designed to ramp up scrutiny of lenders' activities involving digital assets and blockchain technologies. Now, under pro-crypto President Donald Trump, agencies are working to provide a comprehensive regulatory framework.

data.title: SA Asks: Can Tesla's robotaxi service beat Waymo?
data.description:
Can Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) highly anticipated robotaxi service beat Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Waymo? 

Seeking Alpha analysts  Brett Ashcroft Green, Bernard Zambonin, Bay Area Ideas and Weebler Finance weigh in. 

Brett Ashcroft Green: This is a question I've contemplated quite a bit. I'm bullish on Waymo (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and have observed their robotaxis all over San Francisco, which very impressively navigated the streets with no one riding shotgun. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) currently has limited geofenced areas in Austin and is quietly slow-rolling everything out. Waymo (GOOG) (GOOGL) seems to be very far ahead at this stage, and in my opinion, their big advantage is the implementation of LiDAR. 

While others will say cameras are all the vehicle processors need to navigate, as per Elon Musk and Nvidia's (NVDA) Jensen Huang, I'm not personally sold on the idea that a vehicle without LiDAR could meet the same safety standards as one with it when it comes to mass transit self-driving taxis. 

I've watched a Waymo pick up and drop off individuals at an extremely congested Golden Gate Park, and the LiDAR sensor on top actually acts as a visible beacon with changing LED images on the spinning LiDAR to show when waiting and when a passenger has entered. It truly feels like a taxicab experience from the ground up, and the beacon itself is an additional safety feature with its signaling to alert passersby.

If Tesla (TSLA) gives in to LiDAR in combination with cameras on the Cybercab model, I think they have a chance to scale up faster. If not, I think Waymo (GOOGL) (GOOG) continues to pull ahead as it multiplies its markets.

You can say the LiDARs are ugly, but you have to ask yourself a question as a rider: would you feel safer with those added visible sensors attached everywhere, or without them? The LiDARs, in a way, are a marketing tool all by themselves.

Bernard Zambonin: In terms of technology, Tesla’s (TSLA) robotaxi could be a step ahead of Waymo.

Waymo (GOOG) (GOOGL) is already up and running in cities like San Francisco, Phoenix, Austin, and Los Angeles, using a sensor-heavy setup with lidar, radar, and 29 cameras for redundancy and safety—hardware that can run into the tens of thousands of dollars per vehicle. 

Tesla (TSLA), on the other hand, is going all in on a vision-only system powered just by cameras (costing only a few hundred dollars) plus its in-house FSD computer, bringing total extra hardware costs down to the low-thousands range. Elon Musk pitches this approach as more scalable, more cost-effective, and closer to how human intelligence works than lidar.

Bay Area Ideas: There's no question that Alphabet's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Waymo is leading Tesla's (TSLA) robotaxi both in terms of technology and reach. Waymo, with its lidar systems, has been able to achieve Level 4 Autonomy, while Tesla still requires safety monitoring. In addition, Waymo already has a major fleet and over a million rides per month spread across multiple cities versus Tesla's handful of cars piloting in Austin. 

Despite trailing Waymo significantly, I believe Tesla has a "secret weapon," which is its business model vision. While Waymo is planning to own their fleet, Tesla's aiming to open up robotaxi service to private owners of cars. This could allow the company to scale rapidly once they achieve higher levels of autonomy and potentially dwarf Waymo very quickly in total number of taxis on the road. Therefore, it's still possible for Tesla to beat Waymo, even though it's currently trailing by a large margin.

Weebler Finance: Despite the hype we see among many Tesla (TSLA) supporters regarding the robotaxi demos, the company's ambitions still appear to be largely conceptual. In fact, the demonstrations indicate they're still far from being fully autonomous.

One of the most frequently raised criticisms against Tesla's (TSLA) robotaxi is its camera-only model to develop self-driving as opposed to leveraging lidar or radar. The idea with a camera-oriented approach is to basically create a single, universal AI driving system that can work anywhere in the world instead of relying on pre-mapped, geofenced areas. 

In theory, this could make the system highly scalable and adaptable. In practice, however, it is likely to result in complications when dealing with the messy and unpredictable situations that drivers encounter (weather variations, unusual road layouts, unexpected hazards or obstructions, etc.)

In contrast, Waymo (GOOG) (GOOGL) has been offering fully autonomous rides to the public for years, all while leveraging lidar, radar, cameras, and HD maps within geofenced zones. The multi-sensor approach ensures a high safety standard while being backed by millions of miles in varied environments. Waymo surpasses most of the Tesla (TSLA) robotaxi's conceptual shortcomings while being a player functioning in a real world setting.

data.title: Wave Life Sciences gains as JPMorgan suspends rating
data.description:
• Wave Life Sciences (NASDAQ:WVE) quickly hit highs of the day, rising 5%, after JPMorgan suspended its rating, price target, and estimates on the company. 
  • Some investors may be speculating that JPMorgan suspended its rating because it's advising the company on some type of transaction.

data.title: American Express credit card charge-offs, delinquency stay solid in July
data.description:
American Express (NYSE:AXP) consumer credit card's delinquency and net charge-off rates were little changed in July even as its lending increased during the month, according to a company filing on Friday.

Its U.S. consumer card delinquency rate was 1.3% in July, unchanged from the prior two months and flat with the year-ago level. The net charge-off rate of 2.0% ticked down from 2.1% and compares with 2.1% in July 2024.

U.S. consumer credit card loans of $93.7B rose 1.2% M/M and 9.1% Y/Y.

For American Express's (NYSE:AXP) U.S. small business card members, the delinquency rate was 1.6%, unchanged from 1.6%. Net charge-off rate was 2.7%, up from 2.6%. While loans rose to $30.5B from $30.1B.

data.title: Short sellers reduce bets against S&P 500 technology stocks in July
data.description:
Short sellers reduced their bets against the information technology sector in July. The sector that holds the highest weightage on the S&P had an average short interest of 1.77% in July, down from 1.89% in June. 

The S&P 500’s information technology sector (NYSEARCA:XLK) has grown more than 15% so far this year, compared to an over 9% rise in the broader benchmark index. 

Enphase Energy (ENPH) was the most shorted stock in the sector in July, while Apple (AAPL) took the spot as the least shorted stock, same as last month.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

Enphase Energy (ENPH), Super Micro Computer (SMCI), and Western Digital (WDC) were the top three most shorted stocks in July. Short interest in ENPH rose to 23.22% in July from 20.97% at the end of June. 

While Super Micro Computer (SMCI) and Western Digital (WDC) had short interests of 17.46% and 9.52%, respectively. 

Apple (AAPL) was the least shorted stock in the sector last month, with a short interest of 0.71%, preceded by Microsoft (MSFT) and NVIDIA (NVDA), with short interests of 0.72% and 0.82%, respectively. 

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

IT Services was the most shorted industry within the information technology sector, with 2.56% short interest in July, down from 2.78% in June.  

Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals was the second most shorted industry with 1.92% short interest as of July 31st, compared to 1.90% at the end of June.  

Communications Equipment was the least shorted industry in the sector this month, with a short interest of 1.37%.

data.title: Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac IPOs could raise mortgage rates - report
data.description:
Allianz SE's Pacific Investment Management Co. warned that the Trump administration's proposal to sell new shares of Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) could result in higher mortgage rates if the debt issued by the mortgage giants loses the government's backing.

Libby Cantrill, head of Pimco's public policy, wrote that unless the sale is structured to preserve the government's commitment to financially support the institutions, investor demand may wane for the mortgage-backed securities that they sell, as reported by Bloomberg on Friday. That would end up making mortgages more expensive for millions of people in an era where home affordability is already a challenge.

Citigroup strategists estimated recently that mortgage rates are likely to increased by 0.1-0.2 percentage points after privatization. It is unclear at this point whether the government would completely privatize Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) through the proposed IPOs that could raise about $30B later this year. 

However, President Donald Trump and some in his administration have espoused releasing the mortgage guarantors from government control, which they've been subject to since the 2008 financial crisis.

Fannie (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie (OTCQB:FMCC) don't originate mortgages. Rather, they buy mortgages from lenders, bundle them and repackage them into mortgage-backed securities, which they sell. The government's support of the securities means that investors demand a relatively small spread for the securities, which helps to keep mortgage rates contained.

Both government-sponsored enterprises ((GSEs)) already trade publicly on the over-the-counter markets. 

As expressed in a note she wrote in May, Cantrill worries that privatizing the GSEs could break the current process. "Don’t fix what’s not broken:  We believe that keeping Fannie and Freddie in conservatorship would ensure stability and liquidity, and is ultimately a better deal for the U.S. taxpayer," she wrote.

Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) stock rose 2.8% and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) shares climbed 1.4% in midday Friday trading.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
